title: Convolutions
blurb: Convolutional layers: the output-size and parameter-count formulas, receptive fields, pooling, 1×1/special convolutions, and why convolutions beat fully-connected layers for vision. Calculation questions here are guaranteed exam points.

knowledge_points.title: Convolution: Output Size & Parameter Count
knowledge_points.recap: A conv layer has hyperparameters kernel $k$, stride $s$, padding $p$. Output spatial size: $H_{out}=\big\lfloor\frac{H_{in}+2p-k}{s}\big\rfloor+1$. Each filter has shape $(c_{in},k_h,k_w)$ and there are $c_{out}$ filters, so **parameters** $= c_{out}\,(c_{in}\,k_h\,k_w + 1)$ (the $+1$ is the bias per filter). Common trios: $(k{=}3,s{=}1,p{=}1)$ keeps size; $(k{=}3,s{=}2,p{=}1)$ halves it.

questions.q: Parameter-count drills (always include bias): (a) input $10\times20\times30\times40$ (N×C×H×W), single $3\times3$ filter, stride 2 — how many parameters? (b) input $16\times32\times64\times64$, single $1\times1$ filter — how many? (c) first conv on an RGB input, kernel $3\times3$, $16$ output channels?
questions.answer:
Parameters depend only on $c_{in}, k, c_{out}$ — **not** on spatial size or stride.
(a) $3\times3\times20 + 1 = \mathbf{181}$ (SS24 2.8).
(b) $1\times1\times32 + 1 = \mathbf{33}$ (SS22 1.3).
(c) $(3\times3)\times3\times16 + 16 = 432+16 = \mathbf{448}$ (SS23 6.1).
questions.extend: Formula: $c_{out}(c_{in}k_h k_w + 1)$. Stride/padding/input-size change the *output dimensions*, never the parameter count. Don't forget one bias per output channel.

questions.q: (a) Conv with 5 filters, size 7, stride 3, padding 1 on input $26\times26\times26$ — output dimensionality? (b) Conv with 4 filters, kernel 5, stride 1, no padding on an RGB image — shape of the weight tensor and its dimensions?
questions.answer:
(a) $H_{out}=\lfloor\frac{26+2\cdot1-7}{3}\rfloor+1 = \lfloor\frac{21}{3}\rfloor+1 = 7+1 = 8$ → output $\mathbf{8\times8\times5}$ (5 output channels).
(b) Weight tensor shape $(4,3,5,5)$ = (output channels, input/RGB channels, height, width).
questions.extend: Output-size formula $\lfloor(H+2p-k)/s\rfloor+1$. Weight tensor = (c_out, c_in, k_h, k_w) — exactly 4 dimensions; naming all four earns the marks.

questions.q: Which statements about a convolution layer's output are true?

options.text: The output size depends on the stride.
All layers in a CNN have the same receptive field size.
The number of parameters depends on the input's spatial size.
Padding can preserve the spatial size of the input.

questions.answer: **True:** output size depends on stride; padding can preserve spatial size. **False:** receptive fields *grow* with depth (not equal across layers), and parameter count is independent of spatial size.
questions.extend: Two independent facts the exam mixes: *output dimensions* depend on $k,s,p,$ input size; *parameter count* depends only on $c_{in},k,c_{out}$.

questions.q: An input $8\times3\times32\times32$ passes through Conv($k{=}3,s{=}1,p{=}1$, 64 filters). Give the output shape and the parameter count.
questions.answer: Output spatial: $\lfloor(32+2-3)/1\rfloor+1 = 32$, with 64 channels → output $\mathbf{8\times64\times32\times32}$. Parameters: $64\times(3\times3\times3 + 1) = 64\times28 = \mathbf{1792}$.
questions.extend: The $(k{=}3,s{=}1,p{=}1)$ 'magic trio' keeps $H,W$ unchanged — memorize it; it's everywhere in ResNet/VGG.

questions.q: (a) For 224×224 RGB input and $N$ output channels, give the CONV1 parameters for Option 1 ($k{=}3,s{=}1$) vs Option 2 ($k{=}11,s{=}4$). (b) What stride makes a $k{=}3,p{=}1$ conv output $8\times8$ from a $32\times32$ input, and what is the downside?
questions.answer:
(a) Option 1: $3\cdot3\cdot3\cdot N+N=\mathbf{28N}$; Option 2: $3\cdot11\cdot11\cdot N+N=\mathbf{364N}$ — the big kernel costs ~13× more.
(b) **Stride 4** gives $\lfloor(32+2-3)/4\rfloor+1=8$. Downside: with stride > 1, the output is **independent of some input pixels** → loss of information / lower data efficiency.
questions.extend: Small kernels are far cheaper; large strides downsample but skip input pixels (information loss).

questions.q: For a conv layer (kernel $k$, padding 1, stride 1, $m$ filters) on input $C\times N\times N$, which affect the **output shape**?
questions.answer: **$N$, $k$, and $m$** affect the output shape (spatial size depends on $N,k$; output channels = $m$). The **input channel count $C$** does **not** change the output shape (it only affects per-filter depth/params).
questions.extend: Output shape = (m, H_out, W_out); $C$ sets each filter's depth but not the output dimensions.

options.text: $N$ (input spatial size)
$k$ (kernel size)
$m$ (number of filters)
$C$ (number of input channels)

questions.q: Which statements about a convolutional layer are true?
questions.answer: **True:** parameter count is independent of input spatial size; stride affects the output spatial size; a $1\times1$ conv changes the channel count. **False:** padding does **not** change the number of learnable parameters.
questions.extend: Params depend on $c_{in},k,c_{out}$ only. Stride/padding change output size, not params; $1\times1$ mixes channels.

options.text: The number of parameters is independent of the input's spatial size.
Stride affects the output spatial size.
Padding increases the number of learnable parameters.
A $1\times1$ convolution can change the number of channels.

knowledge_points.title: Why Convolutions (vs FC) & Equivariance
knowledge_points.recap: Convolutions beat FC layers for vision because they: extract **local** features, accept **variable input sizes**, are **parameter-efficient**, and are **translation-equivariant** (shift the input → output shifts the same way). They remain a **linear** operation. Note: not rotation-invariant in general.

questions.q: Why do convolutions fit semantic segmentation better than fully-connected layers?
questions.answer: FC layers process **global** information from the entire image and require a fixed input size, whereas convolutions operate on **local neighbourhoods**, are translation-equivariant, and accept variable sizes — exactly what a dense, per-pixel task like segmentation needs. Convolutions also use far fewer parameters.
questions.extend: Segmentation needs spatially-localized, size-flexible predictions → fully-convolutional. FC would destroy spatial structure and explode in parameters.

questions.q: State two differences between a convolutional layer and a pooling layer. Does reducing spatial size reduce the parameters of downstream conv layers?
questions.answer:
**Differences:** (1) a conv layer has **learnable parameters**, a pooling layer has **none**; (2) pooling works **channel-wise** (kernel depth 1, each channel independently), while a default conv filter spans the **full input depth**.
**Spatial size vs conv params:** **No** — as long as the spatial size stays larger than the kernel, conv parameters depend on $c_{in},k,c_{out}$ only, so they don't change.
questions.extend: Reducing spatial size cuts *computation* and shrinks downstream **FC** parameters (fixed channel count), but leaves **conv** parameter counts unchanged.

questions.q: For a fully-convolutional network: can you feed images at double the training resolution? Will performance be the same?
questions.answer: **Yes**, fully-convolutional networks accept varying input sizes with no architectural change (the parameter count stays the same). **But performance will not be the same** — convolutions are **not scale-invariant**, so objects at a different scale than training won't be recognized as well.
questions.extend: Size-flexible ≠ scale-invariant. Doubling resolution keeps the math valid but changes the effective object scale relative to the receptive field.

questions.q: Explain the difference between translation *invariance* and translation *equivariance*, and which one a convolution provides.
questions.answer: **Invariance:** the input changes but the output stays the same. **Equivariance:** the input changes and the output changes the *same way*. A convolution is translation-**equivariant** — shift the input and the feature map shifts identically. (Global invariance is approached later via pooling and deep receptive fields.)
questions.extend: Conv = equivariant; pooling adds a degree of local invariance. Neither gives rotation invariance by default.

questions.q: Which statements about CNNs are true?
questions.answer: **True:** convolutional layers are **translation-equivariant**. **False:** low-level features are extracted in **early** (not deeper) layers; kernel size affects parameter count but **padding does not**; convolutions are **not** rotation-invariant.
questions.extend: Conv = translation-equivariant (not rotation-invariant); params depend on $c_{in},k,c_{out}$, not padding; early layers = low-level features.

options.text: Low-level features are extracted in deeper layers.
Both kernel size and padding affect the number of learnable parameters.
Convolutional layers are translation equivariant.
Convolutional layers are rotation invariant.

questions.q: Name one built-in design feature of CNNs that helps with images, and explain why it helps.
questions.answer: Any of: **spatial locality**, **weight sharing**, **translation equivariance**, or **hierarchical feature learning**. E.g. **translation equivariance** — a CNN responds consistently when an object is shifted, which matters because pedestrians/cars can appear anywhere in the frame (autonomous driving).
questions.extend: These inductive biases are exactly why CNNs need fewer parameters and generalize on images better than FC nets.

knowledge_points.title: Receptive Field
knowledge_points.recap: The receptive field is the region of the input that influences one output feature. It grows with depth, larger kernels, and stride. For a chain, $r_{out}=r_{in}+(k-1)\prod(\text{previous strides})$. Three stacked $3\times3$ (stride 1) convs give a $7\times7$ receptive field. Too-small a receptive field → the output can't see enough context.

questions.q: Explain the concept of receptive field. What happens if it is too small? (e.g. 3 consecutive $3\times3$ stride-1 convs — how big is the receptive field?)
questions.answer:
The **receptive field** is the region in the input that affects a particular output feature. If it is **too small**, the output cannot capture information from the whole input, losing global context and hurting performance.
**Example (SS20 P4d):** $1\times1\to3\times3\to5\times5\to7\times7$ — three $3\times3$ stride-1 convs give a $\mathbf{7\times7}$ receptive field.
questions.extend: Each $3\times3$ stride-1 conv adds $2$ to the receptive-field width. Stacking small kernels is the cheap way to grow it (VGG's insight).

questions.q: Compute the receptive field of a neuron after several conv/maxpool layers (e.g. Conv3×3 → MaxPool2×2(s2) → Conv3×3 → MaxPool2×2(s2) → Conv3×3).
questions.answer:
Use $r_{out}=r_{in}+(k-1)\cdot\prod(\text{strides so far})$, stepping forward:
Conv1: $3$ → MaxPool1: $3+1\cdot(2-1)=4$ → Conv2: $4+2\cdot(3-1)=8$ → MaxPool2: $8+2\cdot(2-1)=10$ → Conv3: $10+4\cdot(3-1)=\mathbf{18}$ → receptive field $18\times18$ (SS23 6.2).
questions.extend: Track the running product of strides — each later layer's $(k-1)$ contribution is multiplied by all earlier strides, so pooling (stride 2) accelerates receptive-field growth.

questions.q: Name an architecture change that exactly doubles the receptive field of a conv layer without adding layers.
questions.answer: Increase the **stride of an earlier layer**. E.g. changing Layer 1's stride from 1 to 3 gives receptive field $(6-1)\times3 + 5 = 20$ — doubling it (WS26 P4c). (Alternatively increase an intermediate pooling stride, or enlarge a kernel / use dilation.)
questions.extend: Larger stride or dilation grows the receptive field cheaply but reduces spatial resolution — a trade-off. Pooling layers also increase deeper layers' receptive fields (WS26 1.x).

questions.q: You stack five $3\times3$ stride-1 convolutions. What is the receptive field, and why is this preferred over a single large kernel of the same size?
questions.answer: Receptive field $=1+5\cdot2=\mathbf{11}\times11$. Stacking small kernels is preferred because it uses **fewer parameters** and inserts **more non-linearities** (more ReLUs) than one $11\times11$ kernel with the same receptive field.
questions.extend: VGG's core insight: many small $3\times3$ convs beat one big kernel — cheaper and more expressive.

questions.q: Compare receptive fields after 3 layers: Option 1 (Conv $3{\times}3$ s1 → Conv $3{\times}3$ s1 → MaxPool 2 s2) vs Option 2 (Conv $11{\times}11$ s4 → MaxPool 3 s2 → Conv $5{\times}5$ s1). Which is better for local features?
questions.answer: Option 1: $3\to5\to\mathbf{6}$. Option 2: $11\to19\to\mathbf{51}$. **Option 1** has a much **smaller receptive field**, better for learning **local** features (and far fewer parameters), so it is preferred for early layers.
questions.extend: Stacked small convs grow the receptive field slowly and cheaply; a big stride-4 kernel jumps to a huge RF fast.

knowledge_points.title: Pooling & Backprop through Max-Pool
knowledge_points.recap: Pooling downsamples channel-wise with no learnable parameters. **MaxPool** keeps the max (non-linear, sparse gradient); **AvgPool** keeps the mean (linear, every entry gets gradient). To backprop through MaxPool you must remember the **argmax position** from the forward pass; only that entry gets a gradient (value 1), the rest get 0.

questions.q: MaxPool with $k{=}2,s{=}2,p{=}0$, one unique max per window: what fraction of pixels get a live gradient, and what is its value?
questions.answer: Each $2\times2$ window has 4 entries, only the max gets a gradient → $\frac{1}{4}$ of all entries have a live gradient, and that gradient's value is $1$ (the local derivative of the max w.r.t. its argmax input).
questions.extend: MaxPool routes the upstream gradient entirely to the argmax position. That's why you cache positions in the forward pass — same idea as ReLU caching signs.

questions.q: Given a Max-Pool layer ($2\times2$) on a window with all unique positive values:

options.text: To backpropagate, one needs the positions of the max values from the forward pass.
75% of the derivatives differ from zero.
The layer's weights are updated with the optimizer.
It performs feature selection.

questions.answer: **True:** you need the argmax **positions** to backprop, and MaxPool performs a form of **feature selection** (keeps the strongest activation). **False:** only **25%** (1 of 4) of derivatives are non-zero, and MaxPool has **no weights** to update.
questions.extend: MaxPool: no parameters, non-linear, sparse gradient (1/k² live), selects dominant features. AvgPool would give every entry a gradient.

questions.q: Give one advantage and one disadvantage of average pooling over max pooling. Can we pool without reducing spatial size?
questions.answer:
**Advantage of AvgPool:** every entry of the window receives a gradient (vs only the max). **Disadvantage:** average pooling is a **linear** operation, while max pooling adds **non-linearity**.
**No size reduction:** yes — use a size-preserving trio like $(k{=}3,s{=}1,p{=}1)$, just like with convolutions.
questions.extend: Pooling reduces size only because of stride. With stride 1 + matching padding it preserves resolution while still aggregating locally.

questions.q: Compare how gradients flow back through MaxPool vs AvgPool for a $2\times2$ window.
questions.answer: **MaxPool** routes the entire upstream gradient to the single **argmax** position (the other 3 entries get 0). **AvgPool** spreads the gradient **equally**, giving each of the 4 entries $\frac{1}{4}$ of the upstream gradient.
questions.extend: Max = sparse/selective gradient (needs cached positions); Avg = evenly distributed gradient.

questions.q: Which statements about 2D Average Pooling are true?
questions.answer: **True:** it has **no learnable parameters** and it **reduces the spatial dimensions**. **False:** it does **not** always beat max pooling (max adds useful non-linearity/feature selection), and it does **not** reduce the **channel** dimension.
questions.extend: Pooling (avg or max) shrinks spatial size only, channel count unchanged, no parameters.

options.text: It does not have any learnable parameters.
It always performs better than Max Pooling, since it ignores no features.
It reduces the spatial dimensions of the input.
It reduces the channel dimension of the input.

knowledge_points.title: 1×1 / Special Convolutions
knowledge_points.recap: A **1×1 (pointwise) convolution** mixes channels at each pixel: it changes the channel dimension cheaply (used for bottlenecks, e.g. Inception) while keeping spatial size. Weight shape $(c_{out}, c_{in}, 1, 1)$. Other special types: depth-wise (per-channel), transpose/up-convolutions (upsampling).

questions.q: Explain the role of 1×1 convolutions. Advantage/disadvantage vs 3×3. For a 1×1 conv with 10 input → 5 output channels, give the weight shape and parameter count.
questions.answer:
**Role:** mix/reduce the number of **channels** at each pixel (cheap dimensionality reduction) while preserving spatial size.
**vs 3×3:** advantage — far cheaper in parameters/compute; disadvantage — a **smaller receptive field** (no spatial context).
**Shape/params:** weights $5\times10\times1\times1$, bias $1\times5$ → $5\times10 + 5 = \mathbf{55}$ parameters.
questions.extend: 1×1 conv = a per-pixel fully-connected layer across channels. Inception uses it to cut channels before expensive 3×3/5×5 convs.

questions.q: How can we represent an FC layer with 5 output neurons as a convolutional layer over a $4\times3\times8\times8$ batch? And with a 1×1 convolution?
questions.answer: An FC layer uses **all** input neurons, so the equivalent conv needs a kernel matching the full input: $k_h{=}8, k_w{=}8, k_c{=}3$. Weight tensor $5\times3\times8\times8$ (filters × $c_{in}$ × $k_h$ × $k_w$), output $4\times5\times1\times1$. With a **1×1 conv**, first reshape the input to $4\times(3\cdot8\cdot8)\times1\times1$ and convolve.
questions.extend: FC ⇔ conv with a full-image kernel. This equivalence is why fully-convolutional nets can replace FC classifier heads.

questions.q: In an Inception module, why place a 1×1 convolution *before* a 5×5 convolution?
questions.answer: The 1×1 conv first **reduces the channel count**, so the following 5×5 conv operates on far fewer input channels — drastically cutting its parameters and FLOPs (a 'bottleneck') while keeping spatial size. This made GoogLeNet deep yet efficient.
questions.extend: 1×1 bottlenecks decouple channel-mixing (cheap) from spatial filtering (expensive). Same trick in ResNet bottleneck blocks.

questions.q: Give two reasons to use $1\times1$ convolutions in an image-classification network.
questions.answer: Any two: **reduce channels** while keeping spatial size (save compute / bottleneck); **add non-linearity** without changing spatial size; **build a deeper / higher-capacity model** without changing spatial size.
questions.extend: 1×1 conv = per-pixel channel mixing: cheap dimensionality control + extra non-linearity (the GoogLeNet trick).

knowledge_points.title: Handcrafted Kernels & Feature-Map Tokenization
knowledge_points.recap: We don't use fixed handcrafted kernels (Sobel, Gaussian) as layers because they are **not learnable** and can't adapt to the task — though they can serve as **initialization** or in **augmentation**. A feature map can be reshaped into tokens for a transformer.

questions.q: Why don't we use handcrafted kernels (e.g. Sobel edge detector) inside deep models? Where can handcrafted kernels (e.g. Gaussian blur) still be used?
questions.answer: Because handcrafted kernels are **not learnable** and thus can't adapt to the task — the whole point of deep learning is to *learn* the filters. They can still be used as the **initial weights** of conv layers (then fine-tuned), or in **data augmentation / preprocessing** (e.g. Gaussian blur).
questions.extend: Learnable filters > fixed filters because they specialize to the data. Classical kernels survive only as priors/initialization or as augmentation operations.

questions.q: To feed an intermediate feature map (output tensor of a conv layer) into a transformer to learn pixel relations, how do you reshape the tensor?
questions.answer: Move the channels to the last dimension and flatten the spatial dims: $(B\times C\times H\times W)\to(B\times H\times W\times C)\to(B\times (H\cdot W)\times C)$. Now each pixel — a $C$-dim vector across channels — is a **token** for the transformer.
questions.extend: This 'patch/pixel → token' reshape is the bridge from CNN feature maps to attention (the core idea behind Vision Transformers).

questions.q: Why might initializing a conv layer with a Sobel edge-detector kernel still be useful, even though we don't keep handcrafted kernels fixed?
questions.answer: It gives the layer a useful **prior** (edge detection) to start from; training then **adapts** it to the task. You get faster/better convergence while keeping **learnability** — unlike a frozen handcrafted kernel.
questions.extend: Handcrafted kernels survive only as initialization or in augmentation, never as frozen layers.
